types.name: Symmetric
types.description: The same key encrypts and decrypts. Sender and receiver must share it ahead of time over a secure channel — the eternal chicken-and-egg problem of symmetric crypto. Modern algorithms operate on 128-bit blocks (AES) or as stream ciphers (ChaCha20) and use authenticated encryption modes (GCM, ChaPoly) so both confidentiality and integrity are covered in one step.
pros: Fast — orders of magnitude faster than asymmetric crypto
Compact ciphertext (no padding overhead beyond the IV/tag)
cons: Key distribution is hard — how do you share the key safely?
Doesn't scale — N parties need N(N−1)/2 keys for full mesh
No non-repudiation — both sides hold the same key

types.name: Asymmetric
types.description: Each party has two mathematically linked keys: a public key shared freely, and a private key kept secret. Encrypt with the public key, decrypt only with the private; sign with the private key, verify with the public. This solves key distribution — you can publish your public key on the front page of a newspaper — but the algorithms are hundreds to thousands of times slower than symmetric ciphers.
pros: No pre-shared secret — public keys can travel openly
Enables digital signatures and identity binding
Forms the trust backbone of TLS, SSH, email, signed software
cons: Slow — typically used to bootstrap a fast symmetric session key
Larger keys and ciphertexts (RSA-2048 ciphertext is 256 bytes)
Vulnerable to large quantum computers (RSA, DH, ECDSA)

primitives.name: RSA
primitives.summary: The first widely deployed public-key algorithm. Security rests on the difficulty of factoring the product of two large primes.
primitives.howItWorks: Pick two large primes p, q; let n = p·q. Choose a public exponent e (commonly 65537). Compute the private exponent d such that e·d ≡ 1 (mod φ(n)). Public key is (n, e); private key is d. Encrypt: c = m^e mod n. Decrypt: m = c^d mod n. Signing reverses the roles — sign with d, verify with e.
uses: TLS key transport (legacy — TLS 1.2 and earlier)
Digital signatures on certificates and code
Encrypting symmetric session keys in protocols like S/MIME
strengths: Mature, ubiquitous, well-understood after 40+ years of analysis
Same primitive supports encryption and signing
weaknesses: Slow compared to ECC at equivalent security (RSA-3072 ≈ ECC-256)
Large keys and ciphertexts
Vulnerable to Shor's algorithm on a fault-tolerant quantum computer
Easy to misuse — textbook RSA without OAEP/PSS padding is broken

primitives.name: Diffie-Hellman
primitives.summary: Lets two parties agree on a shared secret over a public channel without ever transmitting the secret itself. Foundational to forward secrecy in TLS.
primitives.howItWorks: Both parties agree on public parameters (a prime p and generator g). Alice picks secret a and sends g^a mod p; Bob picks secret b and sends g^b mod p. Each computes the shared secret: Alice does (g^b)^a, Bob does (g^a)^b — both equal g^(ab) mod p. An attacker watching the wire sees g^a and g^b but recovering a or b requires solving the discrete-logarithm problem. Modern deployments use elliptic-curve DH (X25519, P-256) for smaller, faster keys.
strengths: Forward secrecy — past sessions stay safe even if a long-term key leaks
ECDH variants are tiny and fast
weaknesses: Provides no authentication on its own — vulnerable to MITM unless paired with signatures or a pre-shared identity
Static/finite-field DH at small parameter sizes is broken (Logjam)
Quantum-vulnerable like RSA

primitives.name: Digital Signatures
primitives.summary: A cryptographic proof that a specific private key endorsed a specific message — the basis of certificates, code signing, signed git commits, JWTs, and signed software releases.
primitives.howItWorks: The signer hashes the message and encrypts the hash with their private key — that's the signature. Anyone with the corresponding public key can hash the message themselves, decrypt the signature, and check the two hashes match. If they do, the message hasn't been altered AND it came from someone holding the private key. The hash is essential — signing the raw message would be slow and reveal structure.
uses: X.509 certificates (CA signs the subject's public key)
Code signing — Authenticode, macOS notarisation, Linux package repos
JWT (JSON Web Tokens) with RS256 / ES256 / EdDSA
strengths: Provides non-repudiation — only the private-key holder could have produced the signature
Verification is cheap; one private key can sign for millions of verifiers
weaknesses: Compromise of the signing key invalidates everything ever signed by it
Hash-collision attacks broke MD5- and SHA-1-based signatures
RSA / ECDSA / EdDSA are quantum-vulnerable; post-quantum schemes (ML-DSA, SLH-DSA) are now standardised

primitives.name: Certificates
primitives.summary: A signed document that binds a public key to an identity (a domain, organisation, or person). The whole hierarchy of CAs, intermediates, and leaf certs is called Public Key Infrastructure (PKI).
primitives.howItWorks: A subject generates a keypair and submits a Certificate Signing Request (CSR) — public key plus identity claims — to a Certificate Authority. The CA verifies the identity (Domain Validation, Organisation Validation, or Extended Validation), then signs the CSR with its own private key, producing a certificate. Browsers and OSes ship with a root trust store; any cert chaining to a trusted root is trusted. Revocation is handled by CRLs and OCSP, and increasingly by short certificate lifetimes.
uses: Smart cards, PIV, and YubiKey identity
strengths: Lets a stranger trust an unknown server based on a chain to a known root
Free DV certs from Let's Encrypt commoditised TLS
weaknesses: Trust is only as good as the weakest CA in the trust store — historic compromises (DigiNotar, Symantec) have removed CAs
Revocation in practice is unreliable — soft-fail OCSP, CRL caching
Misconfiguration (expired certs, missing intermediates, name mismatches) is the #1 source of TLS outages
fields: Subject — who the cert identifies (CN, SANs)
Issuer — the CA that signed it
Signature — the CA's signature over the cert body
Extensions — Key Usage, Extended Key Usage, SANs, AIA, CRL distribution

primitives.name: PGP
primitives.summary: Phil Zimmermann's 1991 system for encrypting and signing email and files. Standardised as OpenPGP (RFC 4880, RFC 9580). Uses asymmetric crypto to wrap a per-message symmetric key — the standard hybrid pattern.
primitives.howItWorks: To encrypt a message: PGP generates a random session key, encrypts the message body with a fast symmetric cipher (AES), and encrypts that session key once per recipient using each recipient's public key. The recipient decrypts the session key with their private key, then the body. Signing uses a digital signature over the message hash. Identities are bound by a Web of Trust — users sign each other's keys at key-signing parties — rather than a central CA hierarchy.
uses: Signed software releases (apt, rpm, pacman repositories)
strengths: No CAs needed — decentralised trust model
Long-term identity that survives changes of email provider
Strong, well-vetted cryptography under the hood
weaknesses: Notoriously hard to use correctly — key management is mostly manual
Web of Trust never reached critical mass; most keys are unsigned
No forward secrecy — compromise of a private key decrypts every past message
Modern alternatives (Signal, age) cover most use cases more cleanly

primitives.name: GPG
primitives.summary: The free, open-source implementation of the OpenPGP standard. "GPG" is the tool; "PGP" is the standard (and the original commercial product). Same wire format, same keys, same trust model.
primitives.howItWorks: Ships as the gpg command-line tool plus libraries. Stores keys in a local keyring, manages encryption/decryption/signing operations, talks to gpg-agent for passphrase caching, and integrates with smart cards and YubiKey via OpenPGP card support. Most desktop environments and mail clients (Thunderbird, Evolution, Mutt) drive GPG through GPGME or pinentry.
uses: Signing git commits and tags (git config commit.gpgsign true)
Encrypting backups and secrets (pass, the standard Unix password manager, is built on gpg)
Hardware-backed identity via OpenPGP smart cards / YubiKey
strengths: Ubiquitous on Linux and BSD — already installed on most servers
Full feature parity with the OpenPGP standard
Hardware-key support keeps the private key off disk
weaknesses: Inherits all of PGP's UX problems
CLI is intricate and error-prone — it's easy to encrypt to the wrong key or expose a key fingerprint mismatch
Default settings have improved but legacy keys may still use SHA-1 or short RSA keys
